blocks.text: Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth,
1–2 : , commands to orient one's life around the heavenly reality of Christ's exaltation and authority.
for you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God.
3 : , see 2.11–12n. , expressing an intimate and inseparable connection.
When Christ who is your life is revealed, then you also will be revealed with him in glory.
4 : , at the eschatological judgment.
Put to death, therefore, whatever in you is earthly: fornication, impurity, passion, evil desire, and greed (which is idolatry).
5 : , vehemently refuse to engage in. , inappropriate sexual behavior. , often linked with sexual sins in Jewish and Christian thought (Hos 4.12–19; Rom 1.23–27; 1 Cor 10.7–8).
On account of these the wrath of God is coming on those who are disobedient.
6 : , God's eschatological judgment.
These are the ways you also once followed, when you were living that life.
But now you must get rid of all such things—anger, wrath, malice, slander, and abusive language from your mouth.
8 : , a list of vices commonly condemned in antiquity (cf. 1 Cor 6.9; Gal 5.19–21).
Do not lie to one another, seeing that you have stripped off the old self with its practices
and have clothed yourselves with the new self, which is being renewed in knowledge according to the image of its creator.

9–10 : , another contrast emphasizing the new act of creation (recalling Gen 1.26–27) brought about by baptism (2.12). f, changing one's clothing as a metaphor for adopting a new way of life (3.12–14; Eph 4.22–24).
In that renewal there is no longer Greek and Jew, circumcised and uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave and free; but Christ is all and in all!
3.5–11: Reject sins of the old life.
11 : An eloquent rejection of privileges based on ethnicity and socioeconomic status; cf. the additional abrogation of a gender hierarchy in Gal 3.28. , shorthand in antiquity for uncultured peoples.
As God's chosen ones, holy and beloved, clothe yourselves with compassion, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience.
12 : , virtues emphasizing a proper attitude toward oneself and genuine concern for others.
Bear with one another and, if anyone has a complaint against another, forgive each other; just as the Lord has forgiven you, so you also must forgive.
Above all, clothe yourselves with love, which binds everything together in perfect harmony.
And let the peace of Christ rule in your hearts, to which indeed you were called in the one body. And be thankful.
Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly; teach and admonish one another in all wisdom; and with gratitude in your hearts sing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs to God.
16 : , teachings concerning Christ.
And whatever you do, in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him.
3.12–17: Adopt virtues of the new life.
Wives, be subject to your husbands, as is fitting in the Lord.
18 : , accept a position of subordination. The role of Nympha (4.15n) stands in contrast to the idealized subordinate female.
Husbands, love your wives and never treat them harshly.
18–19 : / , the first of the three fundamental household relationships. Cf. Eph 5:22–33.
19 : , alternatively, “do not be embittered against them.”
Children, obey your parents in everything, for this is your acceptable duty in the Lord.
Fathers, do not provoke your children, or they may lose heart.
Slaves, obey your earthly masters in everything, not only while being watched and in order to please them, but wholeheartedly, fearing the Lord.c
Whatever your task, put yourselves into it, as done for the Lord and not for your masters,
since you know that from the Lord you will receive the inheritance as your reward; you serve the Lord Christ.
For the wrongdoer will be paid back for whatever wrong has been done, and there is no partiality.
3.1–4.6: Ethical implications. 3.1–4: Maintain a heavenly perspective.

3.18–4.1: Household relationships “in the Lord. ” This passage adopts a popular philosophical format of paired commands (wife/husband, etc.) for describing the ancient ideal of the patriarchal family and defends it as appropriate for a community dedicated to Christ as Lord (see the more extensive elaboration in Eph 5.22–6.9). The commands to the male figure somewhat moderate his traditional prerogatives (see 3.19,21; 4.1), although appeal to these rules throughout Christian history has tended to reinforce rather than challenge male authority.
3.22–4.1 : S / , cf. Eph 6.5–9. This relationship is the most fully developed of the three, placing the burden on the slave to act in a way that does not incur the master's displeasure.
